full_response:
Step-by-step reasoning

1. In special (and general) relativity every vector V in space-time is assigned a “squared length’’ (sometimes called its norm) by the space-time metric g_{ab}.  
   In inertial (Minkowski) coordinates with signature (−,+,+,+) the squared length of a 4-vector V^a = (V^0, V^1, V^2, V^3) is  

   g_{ab} V^a V^b = −(V^0)^2 + (V^1)^2 + (V^2)^2 + (V^3)^2.  

2. Depending on the sign of this invariant one distinguishes  
   • timelike vectors: g_{ab} V^a V^b < 0  
   • spacelike vectors: g_{ab} V^a V^b > 0  
   • null (or lightlike) vectors: g_{ab} V^a V^b = 0.

3. For the “null’’ case it is essential that the vector is non-zero; otherwise every zero vector would be null by definition but is excluded by convention.

4. Geometrically a null vector lies on the light cone and is tangent to the world-line of something travelling at the speed of light (e.g. the 4-momentum of a photon).  

Putting this together, the definition is simply:

\boxed{\text{A null (or lightlike) vector is a non–zero 4-vector }k^{a}\text{ whose Lorentzian norm vanishes: }g_{ab}k^{a}k^{b}=0.}

Confidence: 93%
extracted_answer: \text{A null (or lightlike) vector is a non–zero 4-vector }k^{a}\text{ whose Lorentzian norm vanishes: }g_{ab}k^{a}k^{b}=0.
